# What the Architect Owns {#sec-what-architect-owns}

::: {.epigraph}
> *"The first rule of any technology used in a business is that automation applied to an efficient operation will magnify the efficiency. The second is that automation applied to an inefficient operation will magnify the inefficiency."*
>
> — Bill Gates
:::

::: {.column-margin}
**Author's Note:** The final boundary of architecture is accountability. An AI agent can propose, repair, and verify, but it cannot own the risk of failure.
:::

::: {.callout-crux}
An architecture organization can delegate more work, but it cannot delegate away answerability for the resulting decisions. This enforces the **Epistemic Trust Boundary**: AI systems search and propose, deterministic simulators verify and reject, and human architects judge and sign off.
:::

Models, tools, and specialists may perform bounded work, but Architecture 2.0 separates task execution from responsibility. This separation—where human judgment sets the constraints for AI to explore—serves as the ultimate culmination of this book. An AI agent can synthesize a netlist in minutes, but a human architect signs the $10 million tapeout check . AI can absorb the execution burden while human architects own the final outcome (@fig-task-horizon). Named people or organizational roles remain answerable for defining the work, setting the evidence burden, authorizing exceptions, and deciding whether to advance.

```{python}
#| label: fig-task-horizon
#| fig-cap: |
#|   **The task length an AI agent can complete keeps doubling, from seconds in 2019 to a full workday by 2026.** Measured 50%-completion time horizon per model [@KwaEtAl2025MeasuringLongTasks]; the fitted trend doubles about every six months. As agents absorb longer stretches of execution, the boundary the architect must still own, defining the work and answering for the result, becomes the load-bearing one.
#| out-width: "92%"
#| fig-alt: "Log-scale scatter of the task length an AI agent completes at 50 percent success versus model release date from 2019 to 2026. Horizontal reference lines mark one second, one minute, one hour, and one workday. Points rise from a few seconds for GPT-2 in 2019 to several hours by 2026 along a fitted line that doubles about every six months, passing through GPT-4 and Claude 3.7 Sonnet at about one hour."

import csv
import datetime
from pathlib import Path

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import _python.arch2_plots as _ap
from _python.arch2_plots import COLORS, apply_style
from _python.labels import place_labels

apply_style()

# Data: METR time-horizon benchmark [@KwaEtAl2025MeasuringLongTasks]; 50%
# task-completion horizon per model. Doubling time ~188 days (all-time stitched
# estimate, from the source). Values dataset-transcribed. Accessed 2026-07-19.
# Receipt: data/source-receipts/chapter11-task-horizon.csv
_root = Path(_ap.__file__).resolve().parents[2]
double_days = 187.778

def _yfrac(text):
    d = datetime.datetime.strptime(text.strip(), "%Y-%m-%d")
    return d.year + (d.timetuple().tm_yday - 1) / 365.25

pts = []
with open(_root / "data" / "source-receipts" / "chapter11-task-horizon.csv", encoding="utf-8") as fh:
    for row in csv.DictReader(fh):
        try:
            pts.append({"x": _yfrac(row["release_date"]), "h": float(row["p50_horizon_minutes"]), "k": row["model"]})
        except (ValueError, KeyError):
            continue
pts.sort(key=lambda p: p["x"])

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.4, 3.3))
for mins, lab in [(1 / 60, "1 sec"), (1, "1 min"), (60, "1 hour"), (480, "1 workday")]:
    ax.axhline(mins, color=COLORS["row"], lw=0.7, zorder=0)
    ax.text(2018.7, mins * 1.15, lab, fontsize=5.2, color=COLORS["muted"], va="bottom", ha="left", zorder=1)
xs_pts = np.array([p["x"] for p in pts])
hs_pts = np.array([p["h"] for p in pts])
ax.scatter(xs_pts, hs_pts, s=17, marker="o", facecolor=COLORS["designspace"], edgecolor=COLORS["designspace_ink"],
           linewidth=0.4, zorder=3)
slope = 365.25 / double_days
intercept = np.mean(np.log2(hs_pts)) - slope * np.mean(xs_pts)
xline = np.array([2018.9, 2026.5])
ax.plot(xline, 2 ** (intercept + slope * xline), color=COLORS["constraints"], lw=1.6, zorder=2)
ax.text(2020.5, 2 ** (intercept + slope * 2022.0) * 2.3, f"doubling every ~{double_days / 30.4:.0f} months",
        fontsize=6.8, fontweight="bold", color=COLORS["constraints_ink"], rotation=27, rotation_mode="anchor")
ax.set_yscale("log")
ax.set_xlim(2018.6, 2026.6)
ax.set_ylim(0.008, 2500)
ax.set_xlabel("Model release date", fontsize=7.2)
ax.set_ylabel("Task length an agent completes at 50% (min)", fontsize=7.2)
ax.set_xticks([2019, 2021, 2023, 2025])
ax.tick_params(labelsize=6.4, length=2.5, width=0.6)
for _sp in ("top", "right"):
    ax.spines[_sp].set_visible(False)
disp = {"gpt2": "GPT-2", "gpt_4": "GPT-4", "claude_3_7_sonnet_inspect": "Claude 3.7 Sonnet",
        "gpt_5_2025_08_07_inspect": "GPT-5", "claude_opus_4_6_inspect": "Claude Opus 4.6"}
by_key = {p["k"]: p for p in pts}
items = [(by_key[k]["x"], by_key[k]["h"], v) for k, v in disp.items() if k in by_key]
place_labels(ax, fig, items, fontsize=5.0, color=COLORS["ink"])
fig.subplots_adjust(left=0.115, right=0.97, top=0.98, bottom=0.115)
```

The trend is why the boundary matters now rather than later. The length of task an agent can finish unaided has doubled roughly every six months, from seconds of work in 2019 to a full workday by 2026 [@KwaEtAl2025MeasuringLongTasks], steadily enlarging what can be handed off. What does not grow with it is answerability, which stays with the architect however much execution crosses the line.

## Delegation vs. Assignment

This separation distinguishes delegated work from assigned responsibility. Work is delegated when another person, tool, or AI system is permitted to perform it. Responsibility is assigned when a named person or role must set the governing conditions, judge the evidence, authorize the action, and answer for what follows. The term *the architect* denotes an authorized architecture role rather than one heroic individual. Some rights may belong to a lead architect, an implementation owner, a safety or security reviewer, a product authority, or a review body. The key requirement is that those rights and interfaces remain explicit.

This chapter states that requirement as a current engineering rule. A validated check may acquire greater authority when its scope, failure behavior, audit path, and escalation boundary are known. The organization still owns the decision to grant that authority and the duty to revisit it.

::: {.callout-learning-objectives}
After this chapter, you can:

- Distinguish routine automated tasks from final architectural decisions that require human accountability.
- Transfer the complete rationale for a decision, including rejected alternatives, across team boundaries.
- Extract unwritten domain knowledge and convert it into explicit, reviewable constraints.
- Maintain the infrastructure required to preserve the exact lineage and evidence of an automated study.
:::

## The Lighthouse Revisited

If this distinction is real, it should survive the hardest example in the book, which began with a deliberately ambitious prompt.

> Design a low-power, 64-bit RISC-V-based compute subsystem for an
> XRBench-class real-time mobile XR workload. Realize it as a vector-capable
> CPU, accelerator, or SoC block under a 3\ W TDP target in a 3\ nm-class LP
> mobile process, and return a design-space report with evidence and rejected
> alternatives.

The prompt can state an ambition, but it cannot by itself choose the workload and software path, define the represented project state, preserve candidate identity across tools, allocate roles, establish a claim and comparator, decide whether an observation is evidence, stop a weak study, or transfer a result across a new decision boundary. Those omissions are architecture judgments rather than missing prompt detail.

::: {.callout-lighthouse title="The prompt has become a study brief"}
The Lighthouse is no longer a one-time prompt. It is a challengeable architecture study defined by six judgments:

1. **Knowledge and state:** Which sources, assumptions, representations, and current project facts define the design problem?
2. **Execution:** Which actions may alter a candidate, through which versioned environment, and what counts as an observation or failure?
3. **Roles and methods:** Which components propose, predict, search, critique, repair, verify, or execute, and against which conventional alternative?
4. **Evidence:** Which bounded claim is under review, what comparator and check independence it requires, and who may reject or waive?
5. **Study decision:** What did the bounded study establish, fail to establish, or leave unresolved, and why did it stop?
6. **Feedback and transfer:** Which operating conditions permit automation, which later signals can reopen the result, and who owns that revalidation?
:::

As the next decision changes, so do the permitted automated actions, evidence checks, rollback paths, and decision rights. They should change with feedback latency, reversibility, and consequence. Assignment and answerability must survive those changes rather than being inferred anew at every tool or organizational boundary.

Evidence fidelity and decision authority remain separate. A proxy, cycle-level simulator, RTL test, emulator, synthesis flow, place-and-route result, or signoff check may support a claim within its scope. Stronger evidence does not grant a tool permission to approve a waiver or authorize tapeout. The evidence must fit the action, and the right to take that action must be assigned.

## Enforcing the Epistemic Trust Boundary

An architecture study stays coherent only when it rigorously enforces the boundary between search and signoff. Work can move to a model, a script, or a specialist, but the responsibility for the resulting claim stays with a named architect. Few cases test this epistemic boundary as sharply as the public debate over AlphaChip, where a fast and capable placement method invites exactly the confusion the distinction is meant to prevent.

::: {.callout-war-story title="AlphaChip and the Ownership Boundary"}
**The claim.** The public debate surrounding AlphaChip, a reinforcement learning method for chip floorplanning, illustrates the separation between delegated work and assigned responsibility .

**The gap.** It is tempting to credit the AI with designing the chip, which blurs the line between execution and architectural ownership. The agent generated candidate macro placements at superhuman speed, but it did not sign off on the physical limits of the silicon.

**The lesson.** An RL agent can do the work of placement, but it does not own the responsibility for the final physical outcome. When independent reviewers debated the validity of the proxy metrics and the baseline comparisons, accountability fell entirely on the human architects who defined the reward function, selected the benchmarks, and approved the final layouts for tapeout. The agent generated the floorplan; the architect owned the claim.
:::

Separate work from responsibility at the level of actual architecture artifacts. A multi-agent orchestration framework may deploy a team of specialized agents, such as a generator proposing a RISC-V core candidate, a critic debating its vector capabilities, and a verifier checking memory coherence. This autonomous debate does not equal architectural signoff. A script may synthesize the accelerator, a simulator may return power observations against the 3 W target, a validator may reject a malformed layout record, or a specialist may interpret a 3 nm domain constraint.

Crucially, delegating to a script often means encountering tool edge-cases and infrastructure failures.[^fn-eda-edge-cases] None of those automated actions alone selects the XR workload, changes the power objective, recovers from the stalled run, expands the validity region, accepts a waiver, or makes the final architecture decision.

[^fn-eda-edge-cases]: For example, a script may hang for 72 hours because of a FlexLM license checkout failure, or a 3-day simulation may crash due to a full disk.

@fig-architect-owned-boundary separates work that may be delegated from the responsibilities that must remain assigned. The upper band contains substantial technical work. The middle row contains instruments that make the work easier to inspect. The lower band remains visible across the entire study because no artifact, card, check, or replay package owns the resulting decision.

![**Delegated work and assigned responsibility.** Within a defined study, models, tools, checks, and specialists may assist formulation, exploration, implementation, evaluation, explanation, and defense. Named roles remain responsible for scope, abstraction, evidence standards, automation authority, escalation, final approval, ownership transfer, and reopening.](images/F10a-architect-owned-boundary.svg){#fig-architect-owned-boundary width="100%" fig-alt="Architecture study containing the six architecture capabilities, delegated assistance, an architectural claim for review, supporting instruments, and a full-width band of assigned responsibilities."}

@tbl-architect-responsibilities shows the inspectable record that lets a reviewer challenge the assignment. "Assigned" does not mean that the same person performs the work, nor is it satisfied by simply naming an owner on a form. That owner must possess the authority, competence, and access needed to challenge the relevant record.

| **Assigned responsibility** | **Work that may be delegated** | **Inspectable record** |
|---|---|---|
| Define intent, workload, objective, comparator, constraints, and decision boundary | Retrieve precedents, enumerate candidate workloads, calculate metrics, draft a study brief | Bounded architecture question, selected workload and comparator, exclusions, decision owner |
| Approve represented knowledge and current state | Retrieve sources, form embeddings, summarize prior decisions, propose state updates, organize trajectories | Source and state versions, assumptions, missing fields, provenance, update decision, retirement or revalidation condition |
| Authorize actions and accept the execution contract | Generate candidates, call tools, translate formats, run checks, collect observations, retry declared failures | Action schema, candidate identity, environment and tool versions, artifacts, statuses, seeds, variance, cost, access boundary |
| Choose methods, components, roles, permissions, and review budget | Generate, predict, search, optimize, critique, repair, or verify within an assigned role | Role-to-component mapping, conventional alternative, prompt or configuration version where material, permissions, feedback and escalation budget, shared dependencies |
| Set the claim and evidence burden | Run matched comparisons, calculate results, prepare evidence packets, apply validated checks, flag contradictions | Claim, scope, baseline, observations, check independence, limitations, protected-review path, rejection or waiver record |
| Decide, stop, transfer, monitor, reopen, or retire | Summarize the record, propose next checks, prepare handoff and monitoring reports | Decision and rationale, failed alternatives, unresolved work, stopping reason, transfer package, monitoring trigger, new owner |

: **Delegation does not erase assignment.** Work can move among people, tools, and AI systems while the governing responsibilities remain attached to an explicit record. {#tbl-architect-responsibilities tbl-colwidths="[25,35,40]"}

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="Check the delegation boundary"}
In the executed array study in @sec-executed-scale-study, a language model proposed a mapping and mechanism explanation, and a deterministic heuristic provided a conventional comparison. Validators and SCALE-Sim, a cycle-accurate systolic array simulator, rejected or returned observations under a frozen path. The authorized architect still had to accept the workload scope, separate the tie from the contradicted directional prediction, reject the unsupported mechanism explanation, record no change, and decide to stop.
:::

## Ownership and Decision Rights

Execution, acceptance, rejection, and waiver are distinct rights, not synonyms. A role permitted to execute a tool action need not be permitted to accept its result, and a reviewer allowed to reject a candidate need not be allowed to waive the failed check. Furthermore, a specialist may recommend a cross-layer change without owning the product decision. A review body may make the final decision while another role monitors later field evidence and initiates reopening.

This separation matters when work crosses hardware, software, package, and product boundaries. A compiler lead may own whether the current lowering path supports an instruction. A physical-design lead may own a congestion or power integrity rule. A product architect may own a workload or battery obligation. The architecture decision composes those judgments. The essential test is whether the combined assignments leave a gap, an unchallengeable waiver, or the problem of many hands, in which two roles each assume the other owns the transition [@Thompson1980ManyHands].

Ownership transfer is the transfer of an argument rather than just a report. Transferring ownership is not simply passing a snapshot of RTL or a signoff artifact. The new owner needs the full context of the architecture, including architecture decision records, continuous integration pipeline logs, and design review minutes.

This context includes the dissent, the rejected alternatives, and the waivers. The new owner needs the causal chain of why the state is what it is, along with the conditions under which it remains valid. @tbl-ownership-transfer shows what state must move.

| **Transfer object** | **Minimum content that must travel** | **Failure if omitted** |
|---|---|---|
| **Claim and current decision** | Bounded question, workload, objective, comparator, constraints, result, and decision status | Success or failure is repeated without knowing what was actually decided |
| **Knowledge and represented state** | Source and state versions, assumptions, omissions, expert judgments, update history, and known invalid regions | The new owner reasons from stale or incomplete premises |
| **Candidate and execution lineage** | Candidate identity, tool and environment versions, action history, artifacts, failures, seeds, variance, cost, and access limitations | The result cannot be replayed or linked to the evaluated artifact |
| **Method and role composition** | Components, assigned roles, permissions, material configuration versions, conventional alternative, shared dependencies, and escalation path | Later reviewers mistake one composition's result for a method-independent conclusion |
| **Evidence and exceptions** | Claim-to-observation link, baselines, check independence, limitations, counterevidence, rejects, waivers, protected-review status, and unresolved disputes | A bounded acceptance turns into an unqualified pass |
| **Monitoring and reopening** | Later signals, version mapping, rollback or mitigation condition, revalidation trigger, retirement rule, and newly assigned owner | Changed conditions leave an apparently permanent decision with no one able to reopen it |

: **Ownership transfer carries the argument.** A transfer is complete when the new role can locate the open obligations and knows what would invalidate or reopen the decision. {#tbl-ownership-transfer tbl-colwidths="[20,40,40]"}

A transfer can be proportional. A small reversible study need not produce a certification dossier, but it still must preserve enough state for the next authorized person to understand the decision, its limits, and the next obligation. A passing check on a component cannot by itself establish the behavior of the surrounding decision system across organizational interfaces.

The Pentium FDIV case in @sec-stronger-evaluation-expensive showed how a small omission became difficult and expensive to reverse after shipment. The ownership question is different but no less concrete. At each consequential transition, someone had to own the evidence standard, unresolved exceptions, and authority to proceed, and after a transfer the next role also needed the assumptions and open conditions that could still overturn the decision. The public record does not reconstruct every internal handoff, so the case marks where a sound process must assign that responsibility at its technical, product, and public transitions.

## Making Tacit Knowledge Reviewable

The requirement creates an institutional problem known as the irony of automation [@Bainbridge1983Ironies]. Delegating routine work removes the practice through which experts once learned to detect unusual failures. If an AI always handles the memory controller layout or coherence protocol verification, junior architects never develop the tacit knowledge to recognize a congested routing channel or a subtle race condition.

This tacit knowledge includes the implicit assumptions about physical constraints, tool behaviors, and system boundary conditions that keep the system stable. An organization that cannot identify someone who retains this tacit feel for what a check establishes, what it misses, and when it should be challenged cannot responsibly grant that check broad authority. Preserving that feel requires deliberately rotating experts through hands-on studies rather than merely naming an owner.

Formal records solve only part of the transfer problem. Architecture decisions also depend on tacit knowledge, which encompasses the undocumented constraints and context that experienced engineers carry.[^fn-cross-layer-exceptions]

[^fn-cross-layer-exceptions]: For example, a packaging engineer may know that a chiplet partition for the 64-bit RISC-V subsystem threatens the power-delivery mesh. A compiler lead may know that the current LLVM compiler backend cannot efficiently lower a proposed vector instruction for the XR workload. A senior architect may anticipate that the accelerator's coherency traffic will starve the display controller and violate the 3 W TDP target.

Not every judgment can be fully encoded into a schema. Enough of a consequential judgment must be exposed for another person to understand where it came from, where it applies, who validated it, and when it needs another review.

A disciplined elicitation-and-validation process helps make tacit knowledge reviewable (@fig-tacit-extraction-bridge). A model or reviewer may locate, compare, summarize, or elicit relevant judgments from prior reviews, waivers, reports, source code, and domain experts. A named domain owner then validates the interpretation, scope, and consequences. The validated part becomes a sourced project-state entry, an environment constraint, a rejection check, a protected note, or a qualified judgment that remains explicitly human.

![**Reviewing tacit knowledge before using it.** A model can help elicit and organize domain knowledge, but a named domain expert must validate constraints, assumptions, and rejection conditions. Conflicts, expiration, or new evidence require another review rather than a silent overwrite.](images/F10b-tacit-extraction-bridge.svg){#fig-tacit-extraction-bridge width="100%" fig-alt="Diagram showing domain knowledge passing through AI-assisted elicitation and domain validation before entering represented study state, with conflict, expiry, and new evidence returning the record for revalidation."}

Every retained judgment should expose four things: source, scope, owner, and revalidation condition. A constraint tied to one LLVM revision, process design kit, workload mix, or approved waiver cannot silently become a permanent rule. The architect answerable for the larger decision need not perform every specialist task, but must know which judgments can change the result, who validated them, and how they will be revisited.

## Common Objections

Making judgment reviewable sounds expensive, and treated as paperwork it can create false confidence. The objections below each have a direct answer.

**"This overformalizes ordinary architecture work."** The record should be proportional to consequence, reversibility, interface span, evidence weakness, and transfer needs. A local, reversible exploration may need a small study card and replay path. A cross-interface exception or difficult-to-reverse decision needs more. The framework inherits recognizable practices from design-space exploration, multifidelity evaluation, and engineering review; it does not require every study to mimic an aviation safety case .

**"AI may add no value."** This is a possible and useful result. In the executed @sec-running-the-loop study, the model tied the deterministic heuristic, offered a contradicted directional prediction, and gave an unsupported mechanism explanation under one frozen path. The architecture outcome was no change, and the study stopped. A framework that cannot preserve this result without converting it into an AI success claim will misreport a null result as progress.

**"Assignment slows experts and validated automation."** Validated routine checks may reject candidates or advance bounded transitions without convening a broad review every time. Human attention should concentrate on changed scope, weak or protected evidence, exceptions, cross-layer effects, correlated failures, difficult-to-reverse consequences, and ownership transfer. The organization still owns the validation, audit, and escalation boundary of the routine check.

**"Naming an owner creates false confidence."** Assignment does not make a decision correct. It creates an authorized challenge path and prevents responsibility from disappearing between tools, teams, or lifecycle stages. An owner without access, competence, or authority is an unfilled role, not a completed assignment.

**"Protected evidence cannot be made fully inspectable."** Authorized protected review can support an internal decision when the reviewer can inspect and challenge the material. Public claims must remain within the public or independently audited record. Confidentiality does not turn an inaccessible assertion into public evidence.

**"Human authority only reflects current model limits."** The technical boundary may move, but broader machine authority still requires someone to define its scope, validated failure behavior, audit path, exception handling, and authorization. If future systems can assume some of those judgments, the chapter's decision-right distinctions remain useful for stating exactly what moved.

**"The named owner is just a liability sponge."** A person held answerable for decisions they cannot actually supervise becomes a moral crumple zone, absorbing blame for failures the surrounding automation produced [@Elish2019MoralCrumpleZones]. The danger is real precisely when automation runs faster and at higher volume than any person can follow. When agents generate and filter thousands of candidates overnight, human operators lose the situational awareness that oversight assumes [@ParasuramanRiley1997HumansAutomation].

The answer is a genuine capacity check rather than a signature. An owner without the access, time, and independent competence to detect and stop the failures they nominally own is an unfilled role, and naming one changes nothing. This is why the assignment tests later in this chapter ask not only who owns a decision but whether that owner could actually have caught the failure.

Not every architecture problem needs AI, a multi-agent system, a formal loop, or a full study packet. The discipline is to make the claim, scope, evidence, decision, and responsibility proportionate and challengeable when consequential work is delegated.

## Maintaining Shared Infrastructure

The proportional answer depends on maintained resources, which directly addresses three of the field's challenges: the dataset crisis [@GebruEtAl2021Datasheets], the tools infrastructure gap , and reproducibility [@PineauEtAl2021Reproducibility]. Without versioned state, executable environments, checks, and replay records, every team must reconstruct the conditions of a decision from memory, exacerbating the reproducibility crisis. The organization must own more than access to models and tools to bridge the tools infrastructure gap.

It needs a maintained chain from knowledge to execution to evidence to later feedback, overcoming the dataset crisis by treating its own execution history as a curated corporate asset. Shared infrastructure is the system of record, not merely a platform to make AI faster. Its primary purpose is to ensure that when an architecture decision is questioned, the exact state, prompt, toolchain, and context that led to it are reproducible within the limits of vendor IP, tool versions, and license availability.

@tbl-field-maintenance links every shared architecture resource to an owner, a maintenance decision, and a failure mode if it becomes stale or unreviewable.

| **Shared architecture resource** | **What it preserves** | **Required owner and maintenance decision** | **Failure if neglected** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Knowledge corpora, datasets, embeddings, representations, and project trajectories | Sources, design distinctions, prior decisions, failed regions, and current state used in @sec-data-representations-world-models | A qualified owner approves inclusion and update destinations, records source and objective, tests whether important architecture distinctions remain recoverable, and retires stale material | Retrieval or prediction appears current while omitting the state or distinction on which the decision depends |
| Executable environments, action contracts, tool interfaces, candidate lineage, and artifacts | The exact path by which actions changed a candidate and tools returned observations in @sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces | Environment and tool owners maintain versions, access, failure semantics, replayability, and identity across translations | License and environment bit-rot prevents reproduction, a retry silently changes the path, or the evaluated artifact cannot be linked to the candidate |
| Method, component, and role configurations with matched conventional baselines | The composition that generated, predicted, searched, critiqued, repaired, or verified in @sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization | A study or platform owner records the component-role mapping, material configuration versions, permissions, shared dependencies, feedback budget, and comparator | Improvement is attributed to "AI" without knowing which role, composition, cost, or conventional alternative produced it |
| Claims, observations, checks, counterevidence, disputes, waivers, and protected-review paths | The bounded reasoning that turns observations into support, non-support, or unresolved evidence in @sec-feedback-verification-trust | A claim or review owner maintains scope, check independence, access, exception expiry, and reopening conditions | Passing one check becomes universal evidence, a protected assertion becomes a public claim, or a waiver becomes a permanent pass |
| Executed-study packages, negative results, stopping reasons, and replay manifests | What was actually run, learned, rejected, left unresolved, and decided in @sec-running-the-loop | The decision owner accepts the result, preserves material failed paths, and decides whether replay is possible or only partial | Teams rerun dead ends, inflate a no-change result, or cannot distinguish executed behavior from a plan |
| Later field and software-enablement feedback linked to system and submission versions | Evidence on the deployed hardware-software pair and signals that may reopen a decision in @sec-loop-patterns-across-stack | Lifecycle owners maintain version mapping, attribution alternatives, monitoring, rollback or mitigation triggers, revalidation, and retirement | Later gains or failures are assigned to the wrong cause, or stale design evidence survives a changed workload or stack |

: **Infrastructure for cumulative architecture work.** The field needs linked resources that connect a question and representation to tools, feedback, evidence, challenge, and lifecycle ownership. {#tbl-field-maintenance tbl-colwidths="[20,20,30,30]"}

Keep three access modes distinct:

1. **Public comparison:** supports public claims only within material others can inspect or reproduce.
2. **Protected inspection:** permits authorized technical challenge without making the underlying artifact public.
3. **Replay:** reconstructs an executed path when dependencies and access allow; it is not synonymous with public release or independent evidence.

These resources come from public benchmarks, open tools, research artifacts, industrial reviews, telemetry, and synthetic tasks, but an undifferentiated log is not a dataset. A reusable architecture record connects the question, state, action, observation, failed alternatives, interpretation, decision, and later update. The resource may be a corpus, trajectory, embedding, tool environment, evidence packet, or protected archive, but its value comes from preserving those relations and their scope.

Embeddings and learned representations require special discipline. Record their source material, version, objective, and evaluation of whether decision-relevant architecture distinctions remain recoverable. Do not treat them as timeless, neutral infrastructure.

Provenance is also a hard intellectual property (IP) boundary. When using external models or shared infrastructure, architects must track the exact lineage of generated candidates. If an AI proposes a specialized accelerator block, the organization must be able to prove it was generated from authorized sources, not hallucinated from a competitor's proprietary datasheet. Without strict IP provenance, a technically brilliant design may be legally unusable.

## Building Team Competence

Infrastructure does not maintain or challenge itself. Competence requires the ability to build and challenge the entire argument chain as a team, rather than relying on prompt fluency, universal tool mastery, or ceremonial approval. An architect should be able to formulate a bounded question, identify missing state, trace candidate lineage, ask why a method fits the task, separate observation from evidence, challenge exceptions, and reclassify the next decision. Deep specialists remain necessary, and they bear the responsibility of making the tool, model, dataset, assumption, failure mode, and uncertainty legible at the architecture boundary.

A team does not need to build the field's entire infrastructure before it can begin. It needs one bounded architecture question and one challengeable study. The initial record should contain:

1. **Study Scope:** decision, workload scope, objective, comparator, and baseline;
2. **State Representation:** knowledge sources, represented state, assumptions, and missing fields;
3. **Execution Trace:** candidate identity, actions, environment, tool path, observations, failures, versions, and costs;
4. **Method Roles:** model or algorithm roles, permissions, matched conventional alternative, and review budget;
5. **Evidence Burden:** claim, evidence burden, check independence, counterevidence, and protected access where applicable;
6. **Decision Status:** no-change, rejection, waiver, recommendation, stopping, and replay status;
7. **Role Assignment:** roles allowed to propose, execute, reject, waive, recommend, decide, monitor, reopen, and retire; and
8. **Revalidation Triggers:** the later feedback or changed condition that would require revalidation.

A good exercise may end with no architecture change, a failed tool path, or no measurable AI contribution. The observable competence is in diagnosing and recording the result correctly, not in producing a positive result. Competence should be tested on an unfamiliar but tractable architecture problem. Classic quantitative architecture remains the grounding discipline .

The progression in @tbl-team-competence treats the unfamiliar bounded problem as an internal onboarding, qualification, or team-review exercise whose work products can be challenged by another architect.

| **Stage** | **Observable performance** |
|---|---|
| **Begin** | Execute and replay one bounded study; distinguish action, observation, evidence, and decision; document one failure fully. |
| **Develop** | Compare method and role choices, audit a proxy or check, diagnose a cross-layer omission, and transfer a study with its open obligations. |
| **Lead** | Set evidence and authority boundaries across teams, maintain shared infrastructure, rule on exceptions, and reopen or retire claims as conditions change. |

: **Competence appears in the work.** A team demonstrates Architecture 2.0 by completing and defending bounded architecture studies across methods and lifecycles. {#tbl-team-competence tbl-colwidths="[20,80]"}

Teams need enough direct contact with tools, failures, and cross-layer effects to supervise the work they delegate. This continued practice is what answers the irony of automation raised earlier, because retained expertise requires ongoing hands-on work rather than a designation on a form. Education cannot stop at prompting, and expert practice cannot stop at final approval.

## Open Research Questions

**Theme 1: AI-Native Hardware Abstractions**
* How must we radically reinvent hardware description languages and ISAs to be inherently continuous and differentiable, transforming the silicon stack into a native, gradient-friendly search space?
* Can we develop compositional microarchitectural primitives that allow neural networks to directly assemble and optimize data paths in latent space, bypassing traditional RTL syntax entirely?
* If AI writes the hardware, should it also autonomously design the Instruction Set Architecture to perfectly match its generated topologies, obsoleting standardized ISAs?

**Theme 2: The Limits of ML vs. Formal Logic**
* What is the theoretical complexity threshold at which deep learning's probabilistic extrapolation is fundamentally eclipsed by formal logic and deterministic search in NP-hard microarchitectural state spaces?
* Can neuro-symbolic agents effectively partition architectural design spaces, dynamically shifting between heuristic neural search for topology generation and SMT solvers for invariant verification?
* Are there fundamental physical or topological bounds in silicon design that mathematical ML cannot extrapolate without exhaustive simulation?

**Theme 3: The Cognitive Verification Gap**
* When autonomous systems reduce the marginal cost of architectural exploration to near zero, what new formal frameworks are required to prevent human verification and review bandwidth from becoming the ultimate economic choke point?
* How do we engineer interpretability tooling that maps hyper-optimized, "alien" AI hardware topologies back into human-verifiable abstraction layers, ensuring architects retain legal sign-off authority?
* If an agentic system is mathematically proven to be more reliable than a human engineering team, what are the ethical and legal implications of enforcing mandatory human-in-the-loop sign-off?

## Returning to the Architecture Question

::: {.callout-design-principle title="Inalienable Ownership of the Physical Result"}
You can delegate the traversal of the design space, but you cannot delegate the responsibility for the silicon. The human architect retains absolute ownership over the formulation of the question, the selection of the baselines, the rigor of the evaluation constraints, and the final physical consequence of the implemented design.
:::


A model or composed system may help formulate a study, retrieve and organize knowledge, propose and search candidates, execute declared tool actions, return typed observations, detect some contradictions, prepare evidence records, and recommend a next step. It does not thereby select its own workload scope, expand the claim, declare a proxy valid, waive an independent check, reinterpret a failed study as success, or authorize a difficult-to-reverse architecture transition.

This division of labor reinforces the **Epistemic Trust Boundary** (introduced in @sec-feedback-verification-trust)—the absolute boundary between search and signoff. The AI may propose RTL code or architectural parameters, but it is strictly forbidden from altering verification testbenches, physical DRC rule decks, or the target timing constraints. In this relationship, the AI acts as a search mechanism; the architect remains the sole authority over physical and logical correctness.

Because optimization algorithms will exploit any loophole in a scoring metric , the human architect maintains absolute authority to define the evaluation criteria. If an AI generates a passing result for a flawed proxy metric, the resulting timing violation or thermal failure belongs to the architect. They must ensure the metrics used by models strictly align with physical constraints, such as DRC rules, timing margins, and TDP.

This culminates in the core thesis of the book: Architecture 2.0 is not about AI designing chips; it is about humans designing the constraints, bounding the search space, and defining the evaluation mechanisms for AI to explore. The architect retains absolute ownership over the evaluation and final coherence, rather than dictating every keystroke or tool invocation. In the final synthesis, enforcing the Epistemic Trust Boundary means ensuring that:

- **Questions are explicit:** the architecture question and comparator are explicit;
- **State is grounded:** the represented state is sourced, scoped, and current enough for the claim;
- **Traceability is preserved:** the executed candidate and tool path are identifiable;
- **Roles are fair:** the method and role composition fit the task and are compared fairly;
- **Evidence is bounded:** observations become evidence only through a bounded review;
- **Outcomes are visible:** rejection, waiver, stopping, and no-change outcomes remain visible;
- **Feedback reopens claims:** later feedback reopens the result when its scope no longer transfers; and
- **Ownership is assigned:** each decision right and open obligation has a qualified, authorized owner.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="Test the assignment"}
- **Assignment test.** Can a reviewer identify who set scope, authorized automated actions, judged evidence sufficiency, approved any exception, and made the final decision?
- **Capacity test.** Given the named owner's real access, time, and the volume of automated decisions they nominally supervise, could they have detected and prevented the failure, or have we named an owner structurally unable to exercise the responsibility?
- **Transfer test.** Can the next owner find unresolved claims, assumptions, rejected alternatives, waivers, relevant versions, monitoring conditions, and reopening triggers?
- **Challenge test.** Can a qualified person dispute the evidence or interpretation and reach the role authorized to respond?
:::

@sec-appendix-a-bootstrapping shows how to begin with one bounded setup, @sec-appendix-b-design-loop-card supplies the compact study card and review rubric, and @sec-appendix-c-reviewer-checklist classifies the maintained resources.

Architecture 2.0 succeeds not when an AI system emits a design, but when another architect can understand what was decided, challenge why the evidence supports it, identify who held each consequential right, and know what would reopen the decision. That reviewable ownership is what lets the field accumulate defensible architecture decisions and better designs rather than isolated model outputs.
